claims.text: The patient, a 4-month-old Taiwanese female infant with infantile-onset Pompe disease, was found to carry c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) as one of two pathogenic heterozygous mutations in the GAA gene (compound heterozygous with c.424_440del17 [p.Ser142Leufs*29]).

citations.quote: GAA gene sequencing was performed, and the patient had two pathogenic heterozygous mutations, c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29) and c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu).

claims.text: The patient's cross-reactive immunological material (CRIM) status was positive, linked specifically to the c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) allele.

citations.quote: Her cross-reactive immunological material (CRIM) status was positive linked to c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). All above findings indicated the diagnosis of Pompe disease.

claims.text: A one heterozygous pseudodeficiency mutation, p.Gly576Ser, was also identified in this patient alongside the two pathogenic mutations.

citations.quote: One heterogenous pseudodeficiency mutation, p.Gly576Ser, was also noticed.

claims.text: The infant presented with infantile-onset Pompe disease features including mild hypotonia, occasional shortness of breath, cardiomegaly on X-ray, elevated AST (125 U/L), ALT (48 U/L), CK (872 U/L), and severely reduced GAA enzyme activity (0.04 μM/h; normal >0.80 μM/h), with LVMI of 105.49 g/m² (above 95th percentile for age).

citations.quote: Laboratory data revealed abnormally high aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and creatinine kinase (CK), which were 125 U/L (normal range: <32 U/L), 48 U/L (normal range: <33 U/L), and 872 U/L (normal range: 26–192 U/L), respectively. The enzyme activity test of GAA was 0.04 μM/h (normal range: >0.80 μM/h).

claims.text: The variant c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) is described as pathogenic in the context of GAA gene sequencing establishing the diagnosis of Pompe disease.

citations.quote: GAA gene sequencing was performed, and the patient had two pathogenic heterozygous mutations, c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29) and c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). One heterogenous pseudodeficiency mutation, p.Gly576Ser, was also noticed. Her cross-reactive immunological material (CRIM) status was positive linked to c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu). All above findings indicated the diagnosis of Pompe disease.

claims.text: The infant was diagnosed with Pompe disease at birth (ERT started at 15 days old), indicating neonatal/early infantile onset of disease.

citations.quote: She received regular ERT at an initial dosage of 20 mg/kg intravenous infusion every other week beginning at 15 days old until 2 days before surgery.
